home.faq_btn: Still have questions? Visit the FAQ

sobre.title: About "us" (or just me)
sobre.desc: To be honest, the "About Us" in the main menu is just to give the site a corporate portal look. There isn't really a big team behind the scenes here.
sobre.btn: Read the full story, see photos and playlist

carousel.slide1_desc: A small space dedicated to helping people understand and love the Church, its doctrine, and its history.
carousel.slide2_desc: Understand the Catechism, the writings of the Church Fathers, and the eternal truths guarded for over two millennia.
carousel.slide3_desc: See what happens in the Holy Sacrifice of the Altar and understand the liturgical responses.
carousel.slide4_desc: Discover the story of men and women who gave their lives for Christ and find inspiration for your daily walk.
carousel.slide5_desc: Deepen your faith with the daily homily, commented by Fr. Mario Sartori.

livro.subtitulo: A curation of fundamental works for deepening the Catholic faith. From recommended editions of Sacred Scripture to the great treasures of Patristics and Doctrine, explore books that have shaped the Tradition of the Church over the centuries.
livro.biografias: Biographies and Lives of the Saints

autores.sao_joao_paulo_ii: Promulgated by Pope St. John Paul II

patristica_livros.patristica_paulus_32: Paulus Patristics, Volume 32: Faith and the Creed
patristica_livros.sobre_o_livre_arbitrio: On Free Choice of the Will
patristica_livros.enchiridion: Enchiridion (Manual on Faith, Hope, and Love)
patristica_livros.patristica_paulus_7: Paulus Patristics, Volume 7: On the Trinity

historia_livros.civilizacao: How the Catholic Church Built Western Civilization
historia_livros.historia_da_igreja_de_cristo: History of the Church of Christ

doutrina_apologetica_livros.o_amor_que_fez_o_sol_e_as_estrelas: The Love That Made the Sun and the Stars

escolastica_livros.gk_tomas_aquino: St. Thomas Aquinas: The Dumb Ox

espiritualidade_livros.tratado_da_verdadeira_devocao: Treatise on True Devotion to the Blessed Virgin
espiritualidade_livros.ser_ou_nao_ser_santo: To Be or Not to Be a Saint
espiritualidade_livros.as_3_idades_da_vida_interior: The Three Ages of the Interior Life

missa.p1: Participating in Holy Mass on Sundays and Holy Days of Obligation is a {dever} for Catholic Christians ({catecismo2180_2181}). However, far from being a mere formal obligation or a simple social gathering, the Mass makes sacramentally present, in an unbloody manner, the unique and definitive Sacrifice of Jesus Christ on the Cross.
missa.p2: The Mass is not a recent celebration or a "service" created in modern times. It is the fruit of the organic development of a very ancient {rito}, celebrated since the first centuries by Christians. Upon entering the church, we do not merely watch the remembrance of a past event, but we sacramentally participate in the single Sacrifice of Calvary, made present upon the altar.
missa.p3: But do you know what each gesture of the priest means? What do the responses of the assembly, the processions, the liturgical colors, and each moment of the celebration represent? So that we can fully understand and live this sacred mystery, the Church has organized the celebration into liturgical seasons, colors, and rites that guide the faithful, in a pedagogical way, throughout the year.
missa.ciclo_leituras_p1: The Church, to ensure that the people of God have access to all the richness of {sagrada_escritura}, organized the readings of the Holy Mass into cycles carefully distributed throughout the {calendario_liturgico_maiusculo}. These readings are gathered in the {lecionario_romano}, the liturgical book used in celebrations, which organizes the biblical passages proclaimed in each Mass throughout the {calendario_liturgico}. Thus, those who regularly attend the celebrations go through a very large portion of the Bible over a given period, progressively getting to know the entire history of salvation.
missa.ciclo_leituras_p2: {e_por_que} One of the great riches of the Catholic Church is its unity. This means that, anywhere in the world where the same rite is celebrated, the readings proclaimed that day will be exactly the same. Thus, a faithful who attends Holy Mass in Brazil, Japan, Italy, or Nigeria will hear the same Word of God, strengthening the communion among all Catholics spread across the world.
missa.ciclo_leituras_p3: This unity does not happen by chance. It is the fruit of a liturgical organization carefully built over centuries, allowing the whole Church to walk together in meditating on the same Word of God. And this distribution is done through two main cycles:
missa.missa_dominical_p1: In the Masses celebrated on Sundays and in the celebrations that already belong to the Sunday liturgy (such as the anticipated evening Masses of {sabado}), the Church uses a cycle that repeats every three years, known as Years A, B, and C. The goal is that, in each cycle, the community primarily follows an evangelist's perspective on the life and teachings of Christ.
missa.evangelho_joao: And the Gospel of {sao_joao}? Due to its profoundly theological and contemplative character, it does not belong exclusively to a single cycle. Its texts appear every year, especially during {quaresma} and {tempo_pascal}, as well as on other important solemnities of the liturgical calendar.
missa.observacao_ano_liturgico: Note: Since the {ano_liturgico} begins in Advent, the change of cycle occurs before the start of the civil year.
missa.missa_semanal_p1: In the celebrations held from Monday to Saturday, before the start of the Sunday liturgy, the organization is slightly different. The Gospel follows practically the same cycle every year. The First Reading, however, taken from the Old Testament, the Acts of the Apostles, or the Apostolic Letters, is distributed over a two-year cycle:
missa.ano_i: Year I: used in odd-numbered years (2025, 2027, 2029...).
missa.ano_ii: Year II: used in even-numbered years (2026, 2028, 2030...).
missa.missa_semanal_p2: Unlike Sunday Masses, whose main focus is following the Gospels over three years, the weekday cycle seeks to offer a much broader reading of Sacred Scripture. Since it would be impossible to go through so many books in just one year, the Church distributed the First Reading between Years I and II. Thus, those who regularly attend Mass during the week come into contact with a much greater variety of historical, prophetic, sapiential, and apostolic books.
missa.cores_liturgicas_p1: Upon entering a Catholic church, we realize that the celebration has its own visual identity. The colors present in the {vestes_liturgicas}, on the altar, and in various elements of the church are not chosen for aesthetics or personal preference. They are part of the symbolic language of the liturgy and help the faithful recognize, upon entering the temple, exactly where the Church is in the {calendario_liturgico}.
missa.cores_liturgicas_p2: Each color has a specific meaning and visually expresses the spirit of that season or celebration. Just like the music, the readings, and the prayers, the colors also help us live more deeply the mystery celebrated by the Church.
missa.cor_verde_texto: Green symbolizes hope, spiritual growth, and perseverance in the Christian life. It is the color used during most of the liturgical year, reminding us that holiness is built daily, through fidelity to God in the small and great actions of life.
missa.cor_roxo_texto: Violet is the color of penance, conversion, and spiritual preparation. During {advento}, it invites the faithful to prepare their hearts for the birth of Christ. In {quaresma}, it recalls the call to prayer, fasting, and penance in preparation for {pascoa}.
missa.cor_branco_texto: White represents joy, glory, purity, and Christ's victory over death. That is why it is used in the celebrations of {natal}, {pascoa}, the great {solenidades} of the Lord, the {virgem_maria}, the angels, and the {santos} who did not suffer {martirio}.
missa.cor_branco_obs: Note: Although many people think gold is a distinct liturgical color, in the Roman Rite it is used as a substitute for white on particularly solemn occasions. Its meaning remains essentially the same: the glory, majesty, and triumph of Christ.
missa.cor_vermelho_texto: Red recalls the fire of the {espirito_santo} and the blood shed by Christ and the {martires}. It is the color of love taken to the extreme, of the witness of faith, and of the action of the {espirito_santo} in the life of the Church.
missa.cor_rosa_texto: Used only twice throughout the liturgical year, the color rose represents a pause for joy in the midst of the penitential seasons. It reminds the faithful that the great celebration of salvation is drawing ever closer, softening for a moment the penitential character of {advento} and {quaresma}.
missa.cor_preto_texto: Although rarely used today, black remains a liturgical color foreseen by the Church for certain funeral celebrations. It symbolizes Christian mourning in the face of death, without losing hope in the resurrection promised by Christ.
missa.cor_preto_obs: Note: In many dioceses and parishes, violet is used instead of black for All Souls' Day and funeral Masses.
missa.estrutura_p1: Although the Holy Mass is celebrated daily all over the world, it is not a sequence of prayers placed in random order. Every gesture, every response of the assembly, and every rite has a profound spiritual meaning and guides the faithful to the center of the celebration: the Sacrifice of Christ made sacramentally present upon the altar. Currently, the most widespread form of the {rito_romano} is celebrated according to the {missal_sao_paulo_vi}, popularly known as the {novus_ordo}. It is this form of the Mass, present in the vast majority of Catholic parishes, that will be presented below. Its structure is organized into four main parts.
missa.ritos_iniciais_intro: The Introductory Rites open the celebration, gather the faithful as a single community, and prepare them to hear the Word of God and celebrate the Eucharist.
missa.procissao_entrada_p1: In some parishes, there is a custom of ringing the bell to indicate the start of the celebration. At that moment, everyone stands, and the procession with the {ministros} begins.
missa.procissao_entrada_p2: The Holy Mass begins with the Entrance Chant, while the {sacerdote}, the {diacono}, and the other {ministros} walk in procession to the {presbiterio}. Depending on the solemnity of the celebration or the customs of the parish, the {incenso}, the {cruz_processional}, the {velas_processionais}, and the {evangeliario} may be carried.
missa.procissao_entrada_p3: The procession shows that the Church is a gathered and pilgrim people, led by Christ and called to encounter God. More than just the arrival of the {sacerdote} and the {ministros}, it visibly initiates the liturgical action and helps to form a single assembly.
missa.veneracao_altar_p1: Upon arriving at the {presbiterio}, the priest and ministers bow to the altar. The {sacerdote} and the {diacono} kiss it, because the altar represents Christ and will be the place upon which the single Sacrifice of the Cross will become sacramentally present. In more solemn celebrations, the altar and the cross may also be incensed.
missa.veneracao_altar_obs: Note: In many churches, it is common to find {velas_liturgicas} arranged around the altar. They have their own meaning within the liturgy, and their number can vary according to the celebration.
missa.sinal_cruz_saudacao_p1: Next, everyone makes the Sign of the Cross. The priest greets the assembly with one of the formulas provided in the {missal}, such as:
missa.saudacao_sacerdote: The grace of our Lord Jesus Christ, and the love of God, and the communion of the Holy Spirit be with you all.
missa.sinal_cruz_saudacao_p2: This greeting is not a simple “welcome”; it manifests the presence of the Lord in the gathered community.
missa.ato_penitencial_p1: After the initial greeting, the priest invites the entire assembly to humbly acknowledge their sins. This moment spiritually prepares the faithful to partake worthily in the sacred mysteries.
missa.ato_penitencial_p2: The {missal_romano} provides different forms for the Penitential Act. Depending on the celebration, there may be a brief moment of silence for an examination of conscience, followed by the {confesso_deus} prayer (I confess to almighty God) or other formulas provided by the Church.
missa.ato_penitencial_obs: Note: The Penitential Act disposes the heart for the celebration and obtains forgiveness for {pecados_veniais}, provided there is true repentance. However, it does not replace the {sacramento_reconciliacao}, which is necessary for the absolution of {pecados_mortais}.
missa.ato_penitencial_final: At the end, the priest proclaims:
missa.absolvicao_sacerdote: May almighty God have mercy on us, forgive us our sins, and bring us to everlasting life.
missa.kyrie_p1: Next, the entire assembly sings or recites the Kyrie Eleison, a Greek expression meaning “Lord, have mercy.” This is one of the oldest parts of the Christian liturgy and the only prayer in the Order of Mass that traditionally remained in Greek, recalling the origins of the Church.
missa.gloria_p1: After the Penitential Act and the {kyrie_eleison}, when prescribed, the assembly sings or recites the {gloria} (Gloria in excelsis Deo), one of the most ancient hymns of the Christian liturgy.
missa.gloria_p2: Inspired by the angels' song at the birth of Christ (“Glory to God in the highest, and on earth peace to people of good will” — Lk 2:14), this is a great hymn of praise directed to the {santissima_trindade}. In it, the Church glorifies the {pai}, acknowledges the divinity and redemptive work of the {filho}, and invokes the mercy of Christ.
missa.gloria_p3: The Gloria is sung or said on Sundays outside of {advento} and {quaresma}, as well as on {solenidades} and {festas} prescribed by the {calendario_liturgico}. During the penitential seasons of Advent and Lent, it is omitted to highlight the character of preparation and expectation of those periods.
missa.coleta_p1: After the Gloria, when prescribed, the priest invites the entire assembly to pray with a simple call:
missa.coleta_p2: At this moment, everyone is invited to remain in silence for a brief while, presenting their intentions inwardly to God. Then, the priest proclaims the Collect prayer, so called because it "collects" all the intentions and supplications of the assembly into a single prayer directed to the {pai}.
missa.coleta_p3: This prayer varies according to the {calendario_liturgico}, reflecting the mystery celebrated on each day, solemnity, or liturgical season. At the end, the whole assembly responds:
missa.coleta_encerramento: With the Collect, the Introductory Rites conclude, and the Church prepares to hear the Word of God in the Liturgy of the Word.
missa.liturgia_palavra_intro1: Following the Introductory Rites, the Liturgy of the Word begins. In it, it is God Himself who speaks to His people through the {sagradas_escrituras}. The Church proclaims the readings in an orderly fashion to recall the entire history of salvation, culminating in the proclamation of the Gospel, the center of the entire celebration.
missa.liturgia_palavra_intro2: During this part of the Mass, the faithful are not called merely to listen, but to welcome the Word of God attentively and put it into practice in their lives.
missa.primeira_leitura_p1: The Liturgy of the Word usually begins with a reading from the {antigo_testamento}. During the {tempo_pascal}, however, this reading is replaced by the {atos_apostolos}, recalling the mission of the early Church after Christ's Resurrection. On some solemnities and particular celebrations, other readings provided by the {lecionario_romano} may also be chosen.
missa.primeira_leitura_p2: This first reading was chosen to prepare the understanding of the Gospel proclaimed in the same celebration. It often contains prophecies, figures, or events that find their full fulfillment in Christ.
missa.primeira_leitura_obs: Note: In most Sunday Masses and solemnities, the First Reading and the Gospel were chosen to converse with each other. Often, a prophecy, an event, or a figure from the Old Testament finds its fulfillment in the Gospel passage proclaimed on that same day.
missa.leitor_final: At the end of the reading, the reader acclaims:
missa.salmo_responsorial_p1: After the First Reading, the assembly responds to the proclaimed Word through a {salmo}, usually sung. This response manifests the prayer of the Church itself using words inspired by the Holy Spirit.
missa.salmo_responsorial_p2: The refrain is repeated by the entire assembly, while the other verses are proclaimed or sung by the psalmist.
missa.segunda_leitura_p1: On Sundays and solemnities, a Second Reading is proclaimed, taken from the {cartas_apostolicas} or from the Book of {apocalipse}.
missa.segunda_leitura_p2: In it, the Apostles guide the early Christian communities regarding faith, morals, and the life of the Church. During weekday Masses, this reading is normally omitted, leaving only the First Reading and the Gospel.
missa.aclamacao_p1: Before the proclamation of the Gospel, the entire assembly stands to welcome Christ, who will speak through His Word.
missa.aclamacao_p2: During most of the year, the Alleluia is sung, a Hebrew word meaning “Praise the Lord.” In {quaresma}, however, the Alleluia is omitted and replaced by another acclamation, preserving the penitential character of this liturgical season.
missa.evangelho_p1: The Gospel occupies the high point of the Liturgy of the Word and is proclaimed only by the {sacerdote} or the {diacono}. In it, the words and deeds of Our Lord Jesus Christ are announced.
missa.evangelho_proclamacao: A reading from the holy Gospel according to {sao_mateus} / {sao_marcos} / {sao_lucas} / {sao_joao}, depending on the Gospel proclaimed that day.
missa.evangelho_gesto_p1: While responding “Glory to you, O Lord,” the entire assembly makes three small signs of the cross: one on the forehead, one on the lips, and one on the breast. This gesture expresses the desire for the Word of God to illuminate our minds, be proclaimed by our lips, and be kept safe in our hearts.
missa.evangelho_gesto_p2: Throughout the proclamation of the Holy Gospel, the faithful remain standing as a sign of respect and veneration for the Word of Christ.
missa.evangelho_sinais_obs_inicio: Note: After making the three small signs of the cross on the forehead, lips, and breast,
missa.evangelho_sinais_obs_destaque_inicio: one does not make the complete
missa.evangelho_sinais_obs_fim: . This gesture already constitutes the assembly's response to the proclamation of the Gospel.
missa.imagem_beijo_evangeliario: Veneration of the Book of the Gospels
missa.evangelho_final_intro: At the end of the Gospel:
missa.evangelho_beijo: After saying “The Gospel of the Lord,” the priest or deacon reverently kisses the {evangeliario}, praying quietly: “Through the words of the Gospel may our sins be wiped away.”
missa.homilia_p1: After the Gospel, the priest delivers the Homily.
missa.homilia_p2: Unlike the readings, the Homily is not the Word of God, but its explanation and application to the lives of the faithful. Its purpose is to help the assembly understand the proclaimed Scriptures and live the Gospel more fully.
missa.profissao_fe_p1: On Sundays and solemnities, the entire assembly proclaims the {profissao_fe} (Creed). At Holy Mass, either the {credo_apostolos} or the {credo_niceno} may be used, both expressing the exact same faith of the Church.
missa.profissao_fe_p2: By reciting it, the faithful publicly reaffirm the very faith professed by the Church since the first {concilios_ecumenicos}.
missa.oracao_fieis_p1: Concluding the Liturgy of the Word, the assembly presents its intentions to God for the Church, for authorities, for those in need, for the community, and for other particular needs. The reader, the deacon, or another minister proclaims each one of them. At the end of each petition, the assembly responds according to the acclamation indicated for that celebration.
missa.oracao_fieis_p2: These prayers manifest the universal dimension of Christian prayer and prepare the community to begin the Liturgy of the Eucharist.
missa.liturgia_eucaristica_intro1: After the Liturgy of the Word, the Liturgy of the Eucharist begins, the center of the celebration of the Holy Mass. In it, the bread and wine are presented upon the altar and, by the action of the Holy Spirit and the words of Christ pronounced by the priest, they truly become the Body and Blood of Our Lord Jesus Christ.
missa.liturgia_eucaristica_intro2: The Liturgy of the Eucharist comprises the Preparation of the Gifts, the Eucharistic Prayer, and the Communion Rite.
missa.preparacao_oferendas_p1: The bread and wine that will be consecrated are brought to the altar. They represent the gifts of creation and human labor, but also the very lives of the faithful, which are spiritually offered to God and united to the Sacrifice of Christ.
missa.preparacao_oferendas_p2: At this time, in many parishes, a collection is also taken up for the maintenance of the church and the needs of the community. This contribution is a concrete form of participation and charity, but it should not be treated as a fee or a condition for attending Mass.
missa.preparacao_oferendas_obs: Note: Each person should contribute freely, according to their conscience and means. Those who cannot or do not wish to contribute should not feel ashamed or judged.
missa.preparacao_oferendas_p3: The collection may take place at the same time as the preparation of the gifts, but it should not be confused with it: the center of this rite is the bread and wine presented upon the altar. The presentation of the offerings also symbolizes that the faithful place their lives, work, joys, and sufferings before God.
missa.preparacao_oferendas_convite: After preparing the gifts, the priest invites:
missa.orai_irmaos: Pray, brethren (brothers and sisters), that my sacrifice and yours may be acceptable to God, the almighty Father.
missa.receba_senhor: May the Lord accept the sacrifice at your hands for the praise and glory of his name, for our good and the good of all his holy Church.
missa.oracao_oferendas_intro: Next, the priest proclaims the Prayer over the Offerings, proper to the day, and the assembly responds: “Amen”.
missa.oracao_eucaristica_p1: The Eucharistic Prayer is the heart of the entire celebration. It is not a private prayer of the priest, but the prayer of the whole Church, pronounced by him in the name of the assembly and directed to the Father, through Christ, in the Holy Spirit.
missa.oracao_eucaristica_p2: Although there are different formulas for the Eucharistic Prayer in the {missal_romano}, they all contain fundamental elements: thanksgiving, acclamation, invocation of the Holy Spirit, narrative of the institution and consecration, memorial of the Passion and Resurrection, offering, intercessions, and final doxology.
missa.prefacio_intro: It begins with the Preface Dialogue:
missa.prefacio_resposta2: We lift them up to the Lord.
missa.prefacio_gracas: Let us give thanks to the Lord our God.
missa.prefacio_p: These responses invite the whole community to lift up their hearts and consciously participate in the thanksgiving that will be offered.
missa.santo_intro: At the end of the Preface, the entire assembly sings or recites the {santo}:
missa.santo_texto: Holy, Holy, Holy Lord God of hosts. Heaven and earth are full of your glory. Hosanna in the highest. Blessed is he who comes in the name of the Lord. Hosanna in the highest.
missa.santo_obs: Note: The Sanctus may be sung in different melodies and rhythms, but its words remain the same.
missa.santo_p: This hymn unites two biblical passages: the praise of the celestial beings before the throne of God and the acclamation made to Christ upon his entry into Jerusalem. In it, the earthly Church joins the heavenly liturgy to praise the holiness and glory of God.
missa.epiclese_consagracao_p1: Traditionally, the faithful kneel during the Consecration as a sign of adoration of the Blessed Sacrament. The priest extends his hands over the bread and wine and invokes the Holy Spirit ({epiclese}) so that these gifts may become the Body and Blood of Christ. (Note: Unlike in some countries, in the English Rite, the assembly remains in profound silence during the Epiclesis).
missa.epiclese_sacerdote: Make holy, therefore, these gifts, we pray, by sending down your Spirit upon them like the dewfall, so that they may become for us the Body and ✠ Blood of our Lord Jesus Christ.
missa.consagracao_intro: Then, the priest pronounces the words of Jesus at the Last Supper:
missa.consagracao_corpo: TAKE THIS, ALL OF YOU, AND EAT OF IT, FOR THIS IS MY BODY, WHICH WILL BE GIVEN UP FOR YOU.
missa.consagracao_corpo_gesto: At this moment, the priest elevates the Host for the adoration of the faithful and then genuflects in adoration, while an acolyte rings the {sineta_liturgica} during the elevation.
missa.consagracao_sangue: TAKE THIS, ALL OF YOU, AND DRINK FROM IT, FOR THIS IS THE CHALICE OF MY BLOOD, THE BLOOD OF THE NEW AND ETERNAL COVENANT, WHICH WILL BE POURED OUT FOR YOU AND FOR MANY FOR THE FORGIVENESS OF SINS. DO THIS IN MEMORY OF ME.
missa.consagracao_sangue_gesto: At this moment, the priest elevates the Chalice for the adoration of the faithful and then genuflects in adoration, while an acolyte rings the {sineta_liturgica} during the elevation.
missa.consagracao_obs: Note: The ringing of the bell does not “make” the Consecration happen. It simply highlights the moment when the Body and Blood of Christ are presented for the adoration of the faithful.
missa.transubstanciacao_p: At this moment, {transubstanciacao} occurs: although the appearances of bread and wine remain, their substance is converted into the true Body and Blood of Christ. It is not a theatrical representation or a simple symbol: Christ becomes really and sacramentally present upon the altar. Through the words of Consecration and the action of the Holy Spirit, although it is the priest who pronounces the words and performs the visible gestures, he does not act by his own power. By virtue of the {sacramento_ordem}, he acts {in_persona_christi}, a Latin expression meaning “in the person of Christ.” It is Christ Himself who acts sacramentally through the ordained minister; therefore, the bread and wine become the Body and Blood of the Lord.
missa.misterio_fe_intro: After the Consecration, the priest proclaims:
missa.misterio_fe_resposta_intro: The assembly responds with one of the provided acclamations, for example:
missa.misterio_fe_assembleia: We proclaim your Death, O Lord, and profess your Resurrection until you come again.
missa.memorial_oferta_intro: The prayer continues recalling the Death and Resurrection of Christ. (From this point until the Great Amen, the assembly listens and participates silently):
missa.memorial_oferta_sacerdote: Therefore, as we celebrate the memorial of his Death and Resurrection, we offer you, Lord, the Bread of life and the Chalice of salvation, giving thanks that you have held us worthy to be in your presence and minister to you.
missa.unidade_igreja_sacerdote: Humbly we pray that, partaking of the Body and Blood of Christ, we may be gathered into one by the Holy Spirit.
missa.intercessao_igreja_sacerdote1: Remember, Lord, your Church, spread throughout the world,
missa.intercessao_igreja_domingo: On Sundays, is added: "...and gathered here on the day you have made your own (or: on the day Christ conquered death and made us sharers in his divine life);"
missa.intercessao_igreja_sacerdote2: and bring her to the fullness of charity, together with N. our Pope and N. our Bishop and all the clergy.
missa.intercessao_falecidos_sacerdote: Remember also our brothers and sisters who have fallen asleep in the hope of the resurrection, and all who have died in your mercy: welcome them into the light of your face.
missa.comunhao_santos_sacerdote: Have mercy on us all, we pray, that with the Blessed Virgin Mary, Mother of God, with blessed Joseph, her Spouse, with the blessed Apostles, and all the Saints who have pleased you throughout the ages, we may merit to be coheirs to eternal life, and may praise and glorify you through your Son, Jesus Christ.
missa.doxologia_intro: The priest elevates the paten with the Host and the Chalice and proclaims:
missa.doxologia_sacerdote: Through him, and with him, and in him, O God, almighty Father, in the unity of the Holy Spirit, all glory and honor is yours, for ever and ever.
missa.doxologia_p: This is the Great Amen, in which the entire assembly confirms and makes the entire Eucharistic Prayer its own.
missa.pai_nosso_intro: After the Eucharistic Prayer, the assembly prepares for Communion by praying the {pai_nosso}.
missa.pai_nosso_obs: Note: In the Holy Mass, **WE DO NOT RESPOND “Amen”** immediately after the Our Father. The priest continues the prayer with the embolism: “Deliver us, Lord, we pray, from every evil...”. Then, the assembly concludes:
missa.pai_nosso_sacerdote1: ...as we await the blessed hope and the coming of our Savior, Jesus Christ.
missa.pai_nosso_assembleia1: For the kingdom, the power and the glory are yours now and for ever.
missa.pai_nosso_sacerdote2: Lord Jesus Christ, who said to your Apostles: Peace I leave you, my peace I give you, look not on our sins, but on the faith of your Church, and graciously grant her peace and unity in accordance with your will. Who live and reign for ever and ever.
missa.pai_nosso_amem_obs: This is the moment when the assembly responds “Amen.”
missa.rito_paz_titulo: Rite of Peace and Lamb of God
missa.rito_paz_intro: After the prayer for peace, the priest greets the assembly:
missa.rito_paz_sacerdote: The peace of the Lord be with you always.
missa.rito_paz_p1: When indicated, the faithful soberly express the sign of peace to those nearby.
missa.rito_paz_p2: While the priest breaks the consecrated Host, the {cordeiro_deus} is sung or recited, recalling that Christ is the Lamb who takes away the sin of the world.
missa.convite_comunhao_intro: The priest presents the Body of Christ to the assembly and proclaims:
missa.convite_comunhao_sacerdote: Behold the Lamb of God, behold him who takes away the sins of the world. Blessed are those called to the supper of the Lamb.
missa.convite_comunhao_assembleia: Lord, I am not worthy that you should enter under my roof, but only say the word and my soul shall be healed.
missa.convite_comunhao_p: This response takes up the words of the centurion in the Gospel and expresses humility, faith, and trust in Christ's mercy.
missa.comunhao_p1: When approaching Communion, the faithful do not walk forward merely to receive spiritual nourishment or a religious symbol. They approach Christ Himself, truly present in the Eucharist. Therefore, this is one of the moments of greatest recollection and reverence of the entire celebration. At this time, the faithful who are properly prepared, without {pecados_mortais}, receive the true Body of Christ.
missa.comunhao_ministro_intro: Upon presenting the consecrated Host, the minister says:
missa.comunhao_amem_p: This “Amen” means: “Yes, I believe.” Therefore, it must be pronounced clearly before receiving Communion.
missa.comunhao_p2: The Church allows Communion to be received on the tongue or, where authorized, in the hand. The faithful may also receive it standing or kneeling, respecting liturgical norms and the organization of the celebration. In any of these forms, one must approach with faith, recollection, and profound reverence, for it is not common food being received, but the very Body of Christ.
missa.comunhao_obs: Note: Those who receive Communion in the hand must consume it immediately before the minister, without walking away carrying the Host.
missa.comunhao_sugestao: Devotional Suggestion: Before the {eucaristia}, we are in the real presence of Jesus Christ: His Body, Blood, Soul, and Divinity. For this reason, many of the faithful, as an expression of adoration and profound reverence, prefer to receive Holy Communion directly on the tongue and, when possible, kneeling. This is not a gesture of superiority over others, but a personal devotion and contemplation that seeks to outwardly manifest faith in the real presence of Christ. The Church also allows reception in the hand. In either form, what is essential is to approach with faith, respect, and awareness of Who is being received. BUT REMEMBER: THE WAY WE RECEIVE THE EUCHARIST MUST REFLECT WHAT WE BELIEVE ABOUT IT.
missa.oracao_pos_comunhao_intro: When the distribution of the Eucharist is finished, the priest invites: “Let us pray”.
missa.oracao_pos_comunhao_p1: After a brief silence, so that the faithful may give thanks and inwardly guard the gift received, the priest proclaims the Prayer after Communion, proper to the day. At the end, the people acclaim: “Amen”.
missa.oracao_pos_comunhao_p2: With the Prayer after Communion, the Liturgy of the Eucharist, the heart of the Holy Mass, concludes. Strengthened by the Body and Blood of Christ, the faithful prepare for the Concluding Rites, in which they will be sent into the world to live what they have just celebrated.
missa.ritos_finais_intro1: After the Prayer after Communion, the Holy Mass moves towards its conclusion. The Concluding Rites do not represent a simple end to the celebration, but the sending forth of the Church into the world to live what she has just celebrated.
missa.ritos_finais_intro2: Strengthened by the Word of God and nourished by the Body and Blood of Christ, the faithful are sent out to witness to the Gospel in daily life.
missa.avisos_p1: In many parishes, before the final blessing, brief announcements may be made about the life of the community, such as celebrations, events, or pastoral activities.
missa.avisos_p2: These announcements are practical in nature and are not strictly part of the liturgy.
missa.bencao_final_intro1: The priest greets the assembly again: “The Lord be with you”.
missa.bencao_final_intro2: Then, he gives the blessing in the name of the Holy Trinity:
missa.bencao_final_sacerdote: May almighty God bless you, the Father, and the Son, ✠ and the Holy Spirit.
missa.bencao_final_p: Through this blessing, the Church asks God to accompany the faithful in their daily lives, strengthening them to live the faith outside the temple.
missa.envio_intro: After the blessing, the deacon or priest dismisses the assembly with one of the formulas provided by the {missal_romano}, such as:
missa.envio_sacerdote: Go in peace, glorifying the Lord by your life.
missa.envio_p1: This dismissal does not only mean that the celebration is over. It constitutes a true missionary sending: the faithful return to their families, to their work, and to society to put into practice what they have heard in the Word of God and received in the Eucharist.
missa.envio_p2: The very word “Mass” derives from the Latin expression {ite_missa_est}, traditionally translated as “Go, you are sent forth.” Since the first centuries of Christianity, the Church has understood that the celebration does not end inside the temple, but continues in the life of each Christian.
missa.procissao_saida_p1: Just as the celebration began with the entrance procession, it concludes with the recessional procession of the priest and ministers.
missa.procissao_saida_p2: After kissing the altar again as a sign of veneration, the priest leaves the sanctuary along with the other ministers, officially ending the liturgical action.
missa.procissao_saida_obs: Note: The Holy Mass only ends after the priest has processed out.
missa.procissao_saida_p3: Although some people leave the church right after responding “Thanks be to God,” the celebration has not yet concluded. Remaining until the final blessing, the dismissal, and the recessional procession is a sign of respect for the liturgy and gratitude for the gift of the Eucharist.
missa.procissao_saida_p4: Just as we wait for the entrance procession to begin the Holy Mass, we also wait for the recessional procession to conclude it.
missa.estrutura_conclusao: The Holy Mass is the greatest treasure of the Church. In it, Christ makes present the single Sacrifice of the Cross and feeds His people with His Word and His Body. Knowing every gesture, every prayer, and every rite does not mean merely understanding a ceremony, but participating with greater faith, love, and reverence in the greatest mystery of the Christian life.
missa.respostas_sinal_cruz_p: In the name of the Father, and of the Son, and of the Holy Spirit.
missa.respostas_saudacao_p: The grace of our Lord Jesus Christ, and the love of God, and the communion of the Holy Spirit be with you all.
missa.respostas_absolvicao_p: May almighty God have mercy on us, forgive us our sins, and bring us to everlasting life.
missa.respostas_evangelho_p: A reading from the holy Gospel according to...
missa.respostas_palavra_salvacao_r: Praise to you, Lord Jesus Christ.
missa.respostas_oferendas_p: Pray, brethren (brothers and sisters), that my sacrifice and yours may be acceptable to God, the almighty Father.
missa.respostas_oferendas_r: May the Lord accept the sacrifice at your hands for the praise and glory of his name, for our good and the good of all his holy Church.
missa.respostas_coracoes_r: We lift them up to the Lord.
missa.respostas_demos_gracas_p: Let us give thanks to the Lord our God.
missa.respostas_epiclese_p: Make holy, therefore, these gifts, we pray, by sending down your Spirit upon them like the dewfall, so that they may become for us the Body and ✠ Blood of our Lord Jesus Christ.
missa.respostas_misterio_fe_r: We proclaim your Death, O Lord, and profess your Resurrection until you come again.
missa.respostas_memorial_p: Therefore, as we celebrate the memorial of his Death and Resurrection, we offer you, Lord, the Bread of life and the Chalice of salvation...
missa.respostas_unidade_p: Humbly we pray that, partaking of the Body and Blood of Christ, we may be gathered into one by the Holy Spirit.
missa.respostas_igreja_p: Remember, Lord, your Church, spread throughout the world...
missa.respostas_falecidos_p: Remember also our brothers and sisters who have fallen asleep in the hope of the resurrection...
missa.respostas_doxologia_p: Through him, and with him, and in him, O God, almighty Father, in the unity of the Holy Spirit, all glory and honor is yours, for ever and ever.
missa.respostas_embolismo_p: Deliver us, Lord, we pray, from every evil, graciously grant peace in our days... as we await the blessed hope and the coming of our Savior, Jesus Christ.
missa.respostas_embolismo_r: For the kingdom, the power and the glory are yours now and for ever.
missa.respostas_paz_p: The peace of the Lord be with you always.
missa.respostas_convite_comunhao_p: Behold the Lamb of God, behold him who takes away the sins of the world. Blessed are those called to the supper of the Lamb.
missa.respostas_convite_comunhao_r: Lord, I am not worthy that you should enter under my roof, but only say the word and my soul shall be healed.
missa.respostas_bencao_p: May almighty God bless you, the Father, and the Son, ✠ and the Holy Spirit.
missa.respostas_envio_p: Go in peace, glorifying the Lord by your life.
missa.jaculatorias_intro: In addition to the official responses of the Roman Missal, it is very common for the faithful or the priest to pray short prayers, called ejaculations (or aspirational prayers), before or after Mass, or during moments of silence and adoration. These are customs of popular piety and vary from parish to parish.
missa.jaculatoria_santissimo_obs: Usually prayed before the Eucharistic Jesus, at the tabernacle or during adoration.
missa.jaculatoria_santissimo_p: O Sacrament Most Holy, O Sacrament Divine,
missa.jaculatoria_santissimo_r: All praise and all thanksgiving be every moment Thine.
missa.jaculatoria_fatima_p: My God, I believe, I adore...
missa.jaculatoria_fatima_r: I hope and I love You! I ask pardon of You for those who do not believe, do not adore, do not hope and do not love You.
missa.jaculatoria_maria_r: Pray for us who have recourse to thee.
missa.jaculatoria_doxologia_obs: Minor Doxology, widely used to conclude moments of prayer.
missa.jaculatoria_doxologia_p: Glory be to the Father, and to the Son, and to the Holy Spirit.
missa.jaculatoria_doxologia_r: As it was in the beginning, is now, and ever shall be, world without end. Amen.
missa.jaculatoria_miguel_obs: Prayer to St. Michael the Archangel, prayed at the end of Mass in some dioceses.

artigos_religiosos.titulo_p1: Welcome to our Religious Articles section. In the Catholic {tradicao}, many of these sacred objects are given a specific name: Sacramentals. First of all, it is very important to understand that they are not "amulets" or magical objects that bring good luck. The Church firmly condemns any kind of superstition tied to objects, as this diverts our trust away from God ({catecismo2111}). Likewise, the use of medals, crucifixes, and rosaries is not {idolatria}; we do not worship the material of the object, but we use it as a sacred sign that points us toward Heaven (understand in depth about the use of {imagens_sagradas}).
artigos_religiosos.titulo_p2: On the contrary, sacramentals are visible signs, instituted by the Church, that deeply connect us to our faith. As the Magisterium teaches, they imitate the {sacramentos} to a certain extent and exist to prepare our hearts to receive God's grace, sanctifying the most diverse circumstances of our daily lives ({catecismo1667}).
artigos_religiosos.titulo_p3: By wearing these articles or keeping them in our homes, we are constantly reminded that we do not fight alone in our daily {batalha_epiritual}. The devout use of these objects, always united to the {oracao} of the Church, lifts our minds to God, protects us from the snares of the enemy, and places us under the {intercessao} and protection of the {virgem_maria}, the {anjos}, and the {santos} of the {igreja_triunfante}.
artigos_religiosos.cruxifixo_p1: A very common criticism that Catholics hear from Protestant brothers and sisters is that using a crucifix (bearing the image of the suffering Jesus) is to focus on "disgrace," often claiming: "Jesus has already risen, the cross is empty! I do not serve a dead God." While it is a glorious truth that we serve a living God, the empty cross, by itself, was merely a Roman instrument of torture and humiliation. What transformed that cursed wood into the altar of our salvation was precisely the Body of Christ given up for us.
artigos_religiosos.cruxifixo_p2: When we wear and venerate the Crucifix, we are being profoundly biblical. The Apostle Paul said firmly: "We proclaim Christ crucified, a stumbling block to Jews and foolishness to Gentiles" (1 Corinthians 1:23). The Catholic does not deny the {ressurreicao}, but knows that there is no {pascoa} Sunday without the sacrifice of {sexta_Feira_santa}.
artigos_religiosos.cruxifixo_p3: Having a crucifix at home, in the workplace, or hanging on one's chest is the greatest visual reminder of God's infinite love. As the Second Vatican Council teaches: "For it was from the side of Christ as He slept the sleep of death upon the cross that there came forth the wondrous sacrament of the whole Church" ({SC5}). Contemplating Jesus on the cross is not a sign of defeat, but the contemplation of the extremely high price that was paid for our salvation.
artigos_religiosos.cruxifixo_p4: Besides being an object of profound devotion that moves our hearts to repentance, the crucifix is a powerful sacramental of protection in {batalha_epiritual}. {tradicao} and the very Roman Ritual of Exorcisms (which instructs the priest to frequently use the {sinal_da_cruz} and the crucifix during the rite) attest to this power. As the late {padre_gabriele_amorth}, former chief exorcist of the Vatican and author of several books on the subject, taught: the devil has an indescribable terror of the image of Christ crucified, because it is the visual proof of the exact moment and place where his empire of wickedness was eternally defeated.
artigos_religiosos.cruz_petrina_p1: If you follow pop culture or horror movies, you have probably been taught that the inverted cross is a satanic symbol. But this is a huge myth and a recent appropriation! In the true Catholic {tradicao}, this is one of the most beautiful and ancient symbols of our faith: the Petrine Cross.
artigos_religiosos.cruz_petrina_p2: The origin of this symbol dates back to the 1st century. The story that {sao_pedro} demanded to be crucified upside down is recorded by early Christian historiography. The great historian {eusebio_cesareia} (265–339 AD), in his work “Church History” (Book III), preserves the theologian Origen's account of the apostle's death:
artigos_religiosos.cruz_petrina_p3: “Peter, finally having come to Rome, was crucified upside-down, because he had desired to suffer that way.”
artigos_religiosos.cruz_petrina_p4: The memory of this act of humility was so strong in the early Church that even ancient documents from the 2nd century, such as the text Acts of Peter, already recorded the scene. Although this book is an {texto_apocrifo}, great modern academics, such as the German historian {wilhelm} and the archaeologist {margherita} (responsible for identifying the bones of St. Peter in the Vatican), see it as a reflection of a very old tradition that points to the genuine historical memory of the early Christians in Rome: Peter did not feel worthy to die in the same position and with the same glory as Christ.
artigos_religiosos.cruz_petrina_p5: Therefore, the Petrine Cross is the ultimate symbol of supreme Christian humility, reminding us that we must decrease so that Christ may increase. For this reason, the Petrine Cross appears in various contexts linked to the ministry of {sao_pedro} and the {papado} (the direct successor to the Chair of Peter).
artigos_religiosos.terco_p1: Many people, Catholic or not, often hang a Rosary on their car’s rearview mirror or wear it around their neck as a protective necklace. However, the Rosary is not an adornment or an “amulet” that radiates energy. It is, in fact, a sacramental created to assist us in mental and vocal prayer.
artigos_religiosos.terco_p2: The history of the Rosary goes back to the early centuries of Christianity. Monks had the custom of daily praying the 150 {salmos} of the {biblia} (a practice that gave rise to the {liturgia_horas}). Since books were copied by hand and cost a fortune, the laypeople and peasants of the time did not have access to them, nor could they read Latin. For this reason, the Church allowed them to replace the reading of the 150 Psalms with 150 {pai_nosso} prayers (Our Fathers). To keep track of the count, they used pebbles or knotted cords (called {paternosters}). According to historical records in the {enciclopedia_catolica}, this practice was already well-documented and very common by the 11th century in large monasteries such as the famous {abadia_cluny} in France.
artigos_religiosos.terco_p3: This {devocao} evolved organically. The Church always knew that the {ave_maria} prayer is, in fact, profoundly Christocentric (the exact center of the prayer is not Mary, but the phrase “blessed is the fruit of thy womb, Jesus”). Because of this, the faithful began to pray the 150 Hail Marys, meditating on the Incarnation of Christ, in what became known as the “Psalter of Our Lady.”
artigos_religiosos.terco_p4: It was in the 13th century, through {sao_domingos}, that the cord gained the contemplative structure we know today. According to Tradition, the {virgem_maria} herself appeared to St. Dominic and gave him the {rosario} as the ultimate spiritual weapon to defeat the Albigensian heresy (a Gnostic group at the time that taught that matter and the body were evil, denying that Jesus Christ had taken on a real human body).
artigos_religiosos.terco_p5: The entire cord of 150 {ave_marias} formed the {rosario} (a “crown of roses” offered to Mary). Because the {rosario} was long, the cord was divided into three parts of 50 {ave_marias}. Being a third of the original Rosary, the smaller object gained the popular Portuguese name of “Terço” (a third). (Today, with the addition of the {misterios_luminosos} by {papa_joao_paulo_ii}, the complete {rosario} has 200 {ave_marias}, but the common 5-decade chaplet is still used worldwide).
artigos_religiosos.terco_p6: The Rosary is the “Gospel in miniature.” The purpose of passing the beads through our fingers is not to make a mechanical, parrot-like repetition, but rather to mark the time while our minds meditate on the life, Passion, and Resurrection of {jesus_cristo}.
artigos_religiosos.terco_p7: For great saints of the Church, the Rosary is the greatest weapon in {batalha_espiritual}. {sao_pio}, one of the greatest mystics of the 20th century, used to refer to his Rosary by saying: “Bring me my weapon!”. It is no coincidence that in almost all of her {aparicoes} recognized by the Church (such as in {fatima} and {lourdes}), the {virgem_maria}'s most insistent request to humanity has been: “Pray the Rosary every day.”

imagens.catedra_papal: Papal Cathedra (Pope St. John Paul II)
